subject: ap-world-history
theme: The Global Tapestry, 1200–1450: Diverse societies develop distinct political, economic, and cultural systems across Afro-Eurasia and the Americas.

core_concepts.title: Song Dynasty China and Its Influence
core_concepts.summary: The Song Dynasty (960–1279) achieved remarkable prosperity through agricultural innovation (Champa rice enabling double harvests), extensive canal networks, and industrial production (iron, silk, porcelain). Civil service examinations based on Confucian learning promoted merit-based governance and a bureaucratic scholar-gentry class. Song technological innovations — woodblock printing, gunpowder, the magnetic compass, and paper currency — spread across Eurasia and transformed subsequent world history. Though militarily weak against nomadic powers, the Song economy was the world's largest, with sophisticated credit markets and long-distance trade.

core_concepts.title: Mongol Empires and Eurasian Integration
core_concepts.summary: Under Chinggis Khan (r. 1206–1227) and his successors, the Mongols built the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from China to Eastern Europe. After devastating conquests (the sack of Baghdad, 1258, ended the Abbasid Caliphate), the Mongols created a Pax Mongolica — enforcing stability along the Silk Road that enabled unprecedented cross-Eurasian trade, travel, and disease transmission. The four khanates (Yuan China, Ilkhanate Persia, Chagatai Central Asia, Golden Horde Russia) integrated Eurasian economies but also spread the Black Death (bubonic plague) westward after 1346.

core_concepts.title: Islamic World: Abbasid Legacy and Dar al-Islam
core_concepts.summary: The Abbasid Caliphate (750–1258) created a cosmopolitan Islamic civilization centered in Baghdad, patronizing philosophy, mathematics, medicine, and translation of Greek texts. After the Mongol sack of Baghdad, Islamic political authority fragmented among regional sultanates (Delhi Sultanate, Mamluk Egypt, Mali), but the Dar al-Islam — the interconnected world of Muslim traders, scholars, and pilgrims — remained culturally unified. Islamic scholarship (al-Khwarizmi's algebra, Ibn Rushd's philosophy, Ibn Battuta's travels) preserved and expanded human knowledge. Sufi missionaries spread Islam peacefully into sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia.

core_concepts.title: Sub-Saharan Africa: Mali and Swahili Coast
core_concepts.summary: The Mali Empire (c. 1235–1600) controlled trans-Saharan gold-salt trade routes, making Mansa Musa (r. 1312–1337) the wealthiest individual in medieval history. His 1324 hajj to Mecca — with a caravan of 60,000 people and 12,000 enslaved servants — spread Mali's reputation globally. The city of Timbuktu became a center of Islamic scholarship. Meanwhile, the Swahili Coast (Kenya, Tanzania, Mozambique) developed city-states (Kilwa, Mombasa) engaged in Indian Ocean trade, blending Bantu and Islamic cultures and exporting gold, ivory, and enslaved people to Arabia and India.

core_concepts.title: European Feudalism and the Catholic Church
core_concepts.summary: Medieval Europe was organized around feudalism — hierarchical relationships of land grants and military service — with the Catholic Church providing cultural unity and claiming universal authority. The Crusades (1095–1291) represented failed attempts to control the Holy Land but increased European exposure to Islamic goods and learning. The Black Death (1347–1352) killed 30–60% of Europe's population, disrupting feudal labor relations and stimulating economic and social change. Italian city-states (Venice, Genoa, Florence) developed sophisticated banking and merchant capitalism that would enable later European expansion.

core_concepts.title: Americas: Maya, Aztec Precursors, and Andean Societies
core_concepts.summary: Mesoamerican and Andean civilizations developed complex societies independent of Afro-Eurasian contact. The classic Maya (250–900 CE) built sophisticated city-states with advanced mathematics, astronomy, and writing; Maya successor states persisted in 1200–1450. The Mississippian culture (Cahokia) in North America built large ceremonial mounds. Andean societies (Chimú, forerunner to the Inca) organized complex polities using the quipu (knotted string recording). These societies were isolated from Eurasian diseases, which would prove catastrophic after 1492 contact.

key_terms.definition: Fast-ripening, drought-resistant rice variety from Vietnam introduced to China, enabling double harvests and supporting population growth during the Song Dynasty.
Competitive exam system based on Confucian classics used to recruit government officials in China, promoting meritocracy and creating the scholar-gentry class.
Period of relative stability and trade facilitation across the Mongol Empire (c. 1250–1350), enabling safe travel and commerce along the Silk Road.
A Mongol-ruled political unit; the empire divided into four khanates after Chinggis Khan: Yuan (China), Ilkhanate (Persia), Chagatai (Central Asia), and Golden Horde (Russia).
Literally 'house of Islam'; the interconnected world of Muslim communities, trade networks, and scholarship spanning from West Africa to Southeast Asia.
Moroccan Muslim traveler (1304–1368/9) who journeyed over 75,000 miles across the Dar al-Islam and beyond, recording observations in his Rihla (Journey).
Ruler of the Mali Empire (r. 1312–1337) whose legendary pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324 displayed immense wealth and spread Mali's international reputation.
Long-distance trade routes crossing the Sahara Desert, connecting West African gold and salt with North African and Mediterranean markets.
East African coastal cities (Kilwa, Mombasa, Zanzibar) that participated in Indian Ocean trade, developing a hybrid Bantu-Arabic language and culture.
Medieval European political and economic system based on hierarchical relationships: lords granted land (fiefs) to vassals in exchange for military service and loyalty.
Bubonic plague pandemic (1347–1352) that killed 30–60% of Europe's population and 20–30% of the wider Eurasian population, spread westward via Mongol-connected trade routes.
Founder of the Mongol Empire (r. 1206–1227), who unified Mongol tribes and launched the conquests that would eventually span Eurasia.
Educated Chinese elite class defined by success in civil service examinations, who dominated the imperial bureaucracy and local social leadership during the Song–Ming dynasties.
Andean knotted-string recording device used by the Inca and predecessor cultures to store numerical data, tribute records, and possibly narrative information.
City in the Mali Empire (modern Mali) that became a major center of Islamic scholarship and trans-Saharan trade, home to the Sankore University.

exam_tip: AP World History Unit 1 essays frequently ask you to compare two civilizations in the 1200–1450 period. Master the parallel structure: both Song China and Mali were wealthy because of ____, but Song prosperity was based on ____ while Mali's was based on ____. Practice identifying which AP key concept each civilization exemplifies — Song China = innovation and commerce; Mongols = land-based empire building; Mali = trans-Saharan trade; Swahili Coast = maritime trade; Medieval Europe = feudalism and church authority.
